title: Object 35
wikitext:
The Diary
 {{Blurimage|file=DIARY.png|caption=‎}} The Diary is stored in a climate-controlled vault, within a hermetically sealed, transparent display case made of polymer. Only certain researchers are allowed to interact with The Diary. All individuals interacting with The Diary must undergo a psychological evaluation before and after exposure. Under no circumstances are written, printed, or digital materials to be brought within 10 meters of The Diary without prior authorization. Audio or video recordings of The Diary are strictly prohibited, as transcription attempts have shown signs of memetic corruption. In the event of unintended exposure, affected individuals are to be immediately quarantined and monitored for signs of progressive linguistic deterioration or auditory hallucination. If symptoms persist for longer than 48 hours, therapy is to be administered.
Description
 The Diary is a red leather-bound journal, measuring 23cm x 15cm, with no visible publisher markings. Carbon dating of the leather suggests a creation date between 1920 and 1940, though the ink and paper do not match any known historical or regional production methods. Despite appearing aged, the book shows no signs of decay, and pages are resistant to tearing, water, and fire. Upon opening The Diary, subjects report immediate sensory anomalies. These include: * A voice "reading" along in the subject’s native language. * Peripheral motion (shadows, figures) appearing. * Onset of synesthesia, typically involving written language appearing as colors. Text within The Diary varies per subject and reading session. Content ranges from incoherent symbols to fully formed journals, often describing the reader’s own thoughts or memories. Several test subjects have reported the journal predicting events up to 48 hours in advance with impeccable accuracy. Subjects exposed to The Diary for more than 30 minutes begin to experience paranoia and dissociation. After 60 minutes, comprehension of any written language—whether native or learned—begins to deteriorate. In 12% of cases, subjects lost the ability to interpret spoken language entirely, a condition currently irreversible.
Addendum TD-1: Recovery Log The Diary was recovered from an attic in Seileta, Aurielle, in 2014, following reports of four local residents missing under mysterious circumstances. The object was found sealed inside a lead-lined box, accompanied by a note written in a language similar to Aurielleno, roughly translated as: "Seal it with silence, and never let it speak again. Words are wounds."
Addendum TD-2: Incident Log A Date: 2015-09-17 Subject: Dr. Marcus Llewyn Dr. Llewyn insisted on conducting a solo study of The Diary’s pages. After 43 minutes of exposure, surveillance footage recorded him speaking in a language unidentifiable by linguists. He then attempted to write on the walls. Security intervened; Dr. Llewyn resisted and was sedated. Post-incident, Dr. Llewyn showed complete aphasia and an inability to recognize any alphabet, including those he had previously learned. Neuroimaging indicated abnormal activity in certain areas of the brain. Status: Permanently reassigned to long-term psychological care. Exposure time limits reduced to 15 minutes for all researchers.
Addendum TD-3: Audio Log (Expunged) A recorded voice was captured during a test involving a prisoner. Despite no speakers or audio devices present in the room, a faint voice is heard saying: "To read is to be rewritten." Following this event, all tests involving automated audio equipment were discontinued.
Addendum TD-4: Degradation Log On 2020-03-01, Dr. Halvorsen attempted a passive scan of The Diary using a non-invasive neural interface. No physical interaction with the object occurred. However, within 90 seconds, the data feed began returning corrupted data. This was followed by an observable shift in The Diary's file itself on The Database.

Note from Lead Researcher Halvorsen: We believe The Diary is not a book in the traditional sense. It is a linguistic parasite, a predatory structure that uses cognition itself as a host. Every attempt to categorize it—whether verbal, written, or computational—results in structural degradation of the framework used to contain it. This document is likely already compromised by its presence. We are no longer asking "What is The Diary?" We are asking: what language did it come from—and who still speaks it? All further testing has been suspended indefinitely.
[This version has been stabilized. Do not reopen the original file. Do not think in prose. Do not give it your words.]
Researcher’s Journal – Dr. Caleb Myles Internal Archive – Restricted Log (TD-A) I requested access to The Diary’s file for the second time today. First time I read it, I didn’t really feel anything. Just a weird book that made other people go nuts. All of that felt... theatrical. But something’s been weird since then. A phrase in the margins that I don’t remember typing: “What if it’s remembering you, too?” It wasn’t in the official version. I checked. No one else sees it. I see it.
Researcher’s Journal – Dr. Caleb Myles Internal Archive – Restricted Log (TD-A) They told me not to read the earlier revisions. I did anyway. There's a version from 2018, badly corrupted—half-gibberish. But between the lines, there were shapes. Not drawings. Just letters that looked like they wanted to be images. Like they were trying to form something bigger than themselves. It’s 3 a.m. I’ve been rereading the same paragraph for an hour, and I could swear the page layout changes when I blink. My memory is slipping. I forgot my own name badge this morning. I forgot the name of the Director, and I’ve worked here five years. I didn’t forget the phrase, though. “To read is to be rewritten.”
Researcher’s Journal – Dr. Caleb Myles Internal Archive – Restricted Log (TD-A) They quarantined Dr. Mendelson today. She tore a copy of her own research notes into strips and tried to swallow them. She kept repeating something about "clean pages." I haven’t told anyone that I found new entries in my notebook. They’re in my handwriting.
Researcher’s Journal – Dr. Caleb Myles Internal Archive – Restricted Log (TD-A) No one else sees it. Not the margin phrases. Not the small glyphs appearing on the backs of their badges. Not the sound—a soft, constant whisper beneath fluorescent lights. A voice without breath. I asked Dr. Halvorsen what it says. He just blinked at me and said, “What voice?” I think they’re infected. I think I’m the only one who knows it.
Researcher’s Journal – Dr. Caleb Myles Internal Archive – Restricted Log (TD-A) Retrieved from shredded notebook, reconstructed by researchers. "The words aren't in the book. The book is in the words. And now the words are in me. Stop writing. Stop reading. Or it will know your name, too."
Postscript: Dr. Caleb Myles was found unresponsive in his office, surrounded by hundreds of torn paper strips, all blank. No traces of ink. No visible content. His notebook was recovered from his desk drawer. It was completely empty—except for one phrase, written in small block letters on the final page: "I HAVE ACCEPTED YOUR ENTRY."
> OPEN FILE
> OPEN FILE
> OPEN FILE
> the margin speaks
> the book listens
> did it write you too?
> END
'''> BADSUM'''
